title: It is not obvious how to map users' uid on the host to a specific container uid
body:
<!--
---------------------------------------------------
BUG REPORT INFORMATION
---------------------------------------------------
Use the commands below to provide key information from your environment:
You do NOT have to include this information if this is a FEATURE REQUEST

**NOTE** A large number of issues reported against Podman are often found to already be fixed
in more current versions of the project.  Before reporting an issue, please verify the
version you are running with `podman version` and compare it to the latest release
documented on the top of Podman's [README.md](../README.md).  If they differ, please
update your version of Podman to the latest possible and retry your command before creating
an issue.

Also, there is a running list of known issues in the [Podman Troubleshooting Guide](https://github.com/containers/podman/blob/master/troubleshooting.md),
please reference that page before opening a new issue.

If you are filing a bug against `podman build`, please instead file a bug
against Buildah (https://github.com/containers/buildah/issues). Podman build
executes Buildah to perform container builds, and as such the Buildah
maintainers are best equipped to handle these bugs.
-->

**Is this a BUG REPORT or FEATURE REQUEST? (leave only one on its own line)**

/kind feature

**Description**

I am trying to run public rstudio container with podman (without sudo) and work with external data (mapped with -v). The public rstudio container uses uid 1000 internally, which is different than my external user uid. 

I am looking a way to map host uid 1001 to container uid 1000, but could not find from the documentation how this is done.

<!--
Briefly describe the problem you are having in a few paragraphs.
-->

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1.
Run following command with a user having uid 1001.

podman run --name rstudio -e ROOT=TRUE --user 0 --userns=keep-id -v /share:/share --rm -p 8787:8787 -e PASSWORD=foo rocker/rstudio

2.

Try to edit files (with the rstudio) on the /share, which are editable on the host for uid 1001.

3.

**Describe the results you received:**

I can not access the files on the /share (they are editable for user 1001 and rstudio container user is using uid 1000 which is mapped by podman to some other host uid than 1001)

(However, If my uid on the host is 1000 (the same than the "rstudio" user uid in the container), I can access the files on /share just fine.)

**Describe the results you expected:**

I am looking a way to do the mapping like --userns=keep-id, but map my host uid 1001 to container uid 1000 instead of mapping host uid 1001 to container uid 1001.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**



```
$ podman version
Version:      3.0.1
API Version:  3.0.0
Go Version:   go1.14.7
Built:        Thu Jan  1 02:00:00 1970
OS/Arch:      linux/amd64
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```
$ podman info --debug
```

**Package info (e.g. output of `rpm -q podman` or `apt list podman`):**

```
$ apt list podman
Listing... Done
podman/unknown,now 100:3.0.1-2 amd64 [installed]

```

**Have you tested with the latest version of Podman and have you checked the Podman Troubleshooting Guide?**

Yes

**Additional environment details (AWS, VirtualBox, physical, etc.):**

comments.body: There was discussion late last year about a `--userns=image` option (similar to `--userns=host`, but mapping the user specified by the image to the user outside the container). This never happened, but this sounds like a good reason to finally implement it.

Though, it's not clear this would actually work in your case. The explicit `--user 0` means the container is running as root, which would prevent the flag from working as expected. You may need to manually specify userns mappings to do this - I don't have the material on-hand to tell you how to do this, but I will try and find it tomorrow.

I did a test on a Ubuntu 20.04.2 computer.


1. Create the directory _~/share_ on the host
```
esjolund@laptop:~$ mkdir ~/share
esjolund@laptop:~$
```
2. Start `rstudio`


 (The shell variables `rstudioUID` and   `subuidSize` do not have any special meaning outside of this example. They were
just added to make the example easier to read)
```
esjolund@laptop:~$ rstudioUID=1000
esjolund@laptop:~$ subuidSize=65536
esjolund@laptop:~$ podman run \
   -e ROOT=TRUE \
   --user 0 \
   -v ./share:/home/rstudio/share \
   --rm \
   -p 8787:8787 \
   --uidmap=0:1:$rstudioUID \
   --uidmap=$(expr $rstudioUID + 1):$(expr $rstudioUID + 1):$(expr $subuidSize - $rstudioUID - 1) \
   --uidmap=${rstudioUID}:0:1 \
   -e PASSWORD=foo \
   -ti docker.io/rocker/rstudio
```
3. Start Google Chrome and go to http://localhost:8787 
4. Log in with username _rstudio_ and password _foo_
5. File -> New File -> RScript
6. Type some text
7. File -> Save As
8. Click the sub directory _share_
9. Choose `aa` as filename.
10. On the host terminal 

```
esjolund@laptop:~$ ls -l ~/share/
total 4
-rw-r--r-- 1 esjolund esjolund 3 mar 28 18:40 aa.R
esjolund@laptop:~$ 
```

It seems to work!

Thank you! This seems to work. I am trying to wrap my head around these mappings (`"–uidmap=container_uid:host_uid:amount"`). If I understood correctly, host_uids 1... means the range in /etc/subuid and host_uid 0 is the host uid that has executed the podman. So, this says that map all container uids to the /etc/subuid range, except map the container uid 1000 to the host uid who has executed podman.

Oh, I did not understood this from the documentation (the special meaning of host_uid 0). Perhaps there should be plenty of examples (this use-case included).

I tried to rewrite the `--uidmap` section in podman-run.1.md:
https://github.com/eriksjolund/podman/blob/rewrite_uidmap_documenation/docs/source/markdown/podman-run.1.md
(A work in progress...)

I am not absolutely sure, but I think that in most cases users would want podman to automatically map uids and gids to the range specified in /etc/subuid and subgid, except those that user wants to specially map outside of the subuid and subid range. For example, I was simply looking to say "map container uid 1000 to system uid 1001, and map all other uids automatically". I would also think that users would want to map gids (and gid ranges) in a similar manner. Naturally, user should be allowed to map to only own host uid and own host gids.

Btw. With current mapping scheme (only 0 is mapped outside of subuid and subgid ranges), if a user belongs to multiple host groups, say 1001, 1002 and 1003, how can the user map selected container gids to these host gids?

On your BTW: That's not really possible. We rely on the newuidmap/newgidmap executables to set up our user namespace mappings, and they only give us access to the current user (not their supplemental groups) and anything in subuid/subgid. With the keep-groups annotation we can tell the kernel not to drop these groups (so we can still access content owned by them) but they still aren't mapped into the user namespace, so we can't really interact with them properly.

Concerning the documentation, I found @eriksjolund's example very informative telling exactly how to map a certain container UID (1000) to host user's UID (which can be for example 1001) while mapping the rest of the UIDs to the range specified in /etc/subuid. I think the example in a bit generalized form could make a good addition to the documentation.

Something like:
`user@mycomputer:~$ containerUID=1000` 
`user@mycomputer:~$ subuidSize=65536`
`user@mycomputer:~$ podman run \`
`   --user 1000 \`
`   --uidmap=0:1:$containerUID \`
`   --uidmap=$(expr $containerUID + 1):$(expr $containerUID + 1):$(expr $subuidSize - $containerUID - 1) \`
`   --uidmap=${containerUID}:0:1 \`
`   -ti ubuntu /bin/bash`

In addition to adding an example, I think the `--uidmap` command-line option description could also be rewritten. Something like
https://github.com/eriksjolund/podman/commit/5490fb7da031aaa0ed2081c9ee3f2e0e2f9db58c#diff-709c13596091ef13fd9ca751f884bc013c271233f56d3d64a429a90c21b0326e
I could create a pull request (and name it "WIP ...") and also include the example. I guess it will be easier for others to provide feedback when it's in a form of a PR. (It might take a few days for me to create the PR)

I opened up a PR https://github.com/containers/podman/pull/10072 to rewrite the description of **--uidmap**.
(Sorry, I didn't include the example above as I couldn't find the words of how to explain it well).
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
Since we have updated docs, I am closing.
